A crucial point is the issue of capital management systems, which is something everyone in the industry needs to focus on. From this perspective, whether you are a narrowly defined OTC merchant, or someone with related business needs who facilitates matching and facilitating transactions, or someone who uses exchanges to guide users to buy crypto assets and then transfers or uses them for other purposes, the risk is too high, and we strongly advise against it. At the same time, we also see some restrictions on intermediaries and related service groups. I think this is quite business-oriented, because the reason this regulatory document is so detailed is because it focuses on and describes many micro-level scenarios within the industry. For example, it even mentions that: virtual currencies and related financial products cannot be included in the scope of collateral or pledges; and that insurance businesses related to virtual currencies cannot be conducted, etc. I don't know how everyone will understand this statement. Because we know that some people in the OTC market are now using a different phrasing: "I'm lending you money, but I'll use cryptocurrency as collateral." I don't know if everyone understands this meaning. In traditional trading, it's a cash-on-delivery transaction, which falls under the category of cryptocurrency trading. But now some people are getting "savvy," and they might want to make an adjustment: "I'm lending you money, a pure lending relationship, but I'm worried about your repayment risk. You happen to have some economically valuable cryptocurrency assets, and you provide them to me as collateral through pledging or other means. I'm not helping you exchange cryptocurrency for fiat currency; it's actually a lending relationship." This is a very subtle scenario. But can you imagine? A description like this is actually included directly in such regulatory documents. So from this perspective, we can understand to some extent that regulatory authorities are quite familiar with the entire cryptocurrency industry, especially with some of the tricks and tactics used in mainland China. Conversely, this is also because many insiders or industry partners describe and explain many business details when facing regulatory authorities. So don't think that just because you're being clever, the relevant departments won't understand or see your tricks; that's highly unlikely. I think this document does have many points that can be discussed and studied in detail, which is why I'm using a relatively long short video to talk about this. The seventh point is "strengthening the management of internet information content and access." Everyone should understand this point, because currently on many short video media platforms, such as Douyin, Douyin, and WeChat Video, we see many overseas exchanges or project teams using a large number of "alias" accounts to promote themselves, and even recruit. The platforms will also, according to the requirements of the Cyberspace Administration of China, regularly tighten restrictions on some sensitive words and control related accounts. Regulatory authorities have already noticed this trend, and therefore, the regulatory requirements for these internet platforms are becoming increasingly stringent. When law enforcement collaboration is involved, relevant internet platforms or content platforms also need to cooperate. The eighth point is "strengthening the registration and advertising management of business entities." Here, I'd like to discuss two details. The first detail concerns Khorgos in Xinjiang, where there is a free trade zone. From what I understand, a couple of years ago, when the local management committee issued business licenses to some companies within its jurisdiction, the scope of business included "judicial disposal of cryptocurrencies." Therefore, such regulations or documents specifically target this type of detail. Many free trade zones might think they can pilot some financial innovations within their zones. However, this document actually directly expresses a negative attitude. Another small detail is that we know last year, some investment products related to cryptocurrencies appeared on a certain platform. Of course, it sold out on the same day and was then taken down. That financial product was essentially an investment fund. If I remember correctly, about 10% of its underlying assets were invested in Bitcoin ETFs and some crypto-asset stocks, such as Coinbase shares. So strictly speaking, it wasn't a cryptocurrency fund product as we understand it. However, advertisements for investing in related assets through this multi-layered nesting method are also subject to regulatory restrictions. Therefore, it was also taken down and cannot be advertised or promoted. The ninth point concerns the crackdown on cryptocurrency mining activities. A particularly important point here is that, in addition to the emphasis since 2021 that any cryptocurrency mining discovered in mainland China must be shut down and no new mining is allowed, this is also a response to the resurgence of cryptocurrency mining activities in mainland China over the past year. We know that mainland China once accounted for 70% of the global Bitcoin hashrate, with very high output. However, after 2021, theoretically, all of China's cryptocurrency mining has moved overseas. However, according to lawyer Hong Lin's observations over the past two years, from a statistical perspective, China still possesses at least 20% of the computing power in Bitcoin mining. In the past six months, I've seen many people directly selling mining machines and even providing mining machine hosting services in news media, such as on social media platforms like Douyin, and in my WeChat Moments. Of course, these things are often well-hidden, for example, within cloud computing centers and computing power centers in various locations. Some may be more blatant, thinking that the government's attitude on this issue has softened. But in reality, this is a reaffirmation and emphasis: it's not allowed. Another point I think everyone needs to pay special attention to is: mining machine manufacturers are strictly prohibited from providing mining machine sales and other services within China. This is essentially saying that mining is prohibited, and selling mining machines within the mainland is also prohibited. Because I often see people selling mining machines on WeChat Moments. This essentially means that mining machines can only be exported from now on, not sold in mainland China. Those selling mining machines, hardware, or so-called "nodes" or "servers" under the guise of "DePin" on WeChat Moments need to pay special attention. There's a misconception that selling mining machines for other minor cryptocurrencies is acceptable, as long as it's not Bitcoin mining. However, regulators are aware of this and are cracking down on such borderline activities. Later, it will be mentioned that besides illegal financial activities, if criminal offenses are involved, the case will be transferred to the police; we won't elaborate on that. Strengthening industry self-regulation will also be discussed in detail. The fourth part, which is the main focus, concerns RWA, etc. This document clearly defines that if you are a domestic entity and want to conduct related activities in mainland China under the name of RWA, this is not allowed. If you are a domestic company or project and want to issue related RW tokens directly overseas, it's not possible. However, there is a small loophole: if you have obtained approval or filing with the relevant regulatory authorities, you can then conduct related operations overseas. This echoes or corresponds to the China Securities Regulatory Commission's (CSRC) Document No. 1 of 2026. This document states that if a domestic entity or project wants to issue RWA products overseas, firstly, you definitely need to file with the CSRC in China. While it's not an approval process, I think its authority is slightly weaker, but the threshold and requirements are still quite high. Secondly, if you want to issue RWA products overseas, you must find an overseas subsidiary or branch of a domestic financial institution. There are also requirements regarding some business details, but I understand it's basically the same logic and standards as overseas bond issuance. Therefore, these points clearly define the boundaries of what is permissible and impermissible in the Web3 industry's understanding of the RWA field. The restrictions are as follows: If the assets are domestic, you cannot directly issue related tokens within China; if the assets are domestic, when issuing RWA tokens overseas, you cannot target mainland Chinese consumers; even if your project and assets are overseas, you cannot sell them to mainland Chinese residents. The restrictions are as follows: After filing with the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) in China, you can find overseas subsidiaries or branches of domestic financial institutions to provide the relevant issuance services. Furthermore, all information regarding the entire product and project operation process must be promptly shared with the regulatory authorities. This is essentially what we see as what is permissible and impermissible in the RWA field. Therefore, the CSRC's Document No. 1 describes some negative lists (i.e., what cannot be done, which companies cannot do, and which individuals cannot do). Although its wording and expression do not use the term "RWA," I understand this to be a cautious innovation. This morning, a reporter interviewed me, asking for my views on this matter. I believe that, under the current circumstances, China's efforts to address the issue of high-quality assets on the mainland, or rather, the development direction the country wants to encourage, can be achieved through securities tokenization. On the one hand, this can support Hong Kong's financial innovation and industrial applications in Web3. On the other hand, it can be considered a cautious financial innovation under the supervision of the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC). However, it may be very far removed from what our industry partners want to achieve. This is basically not something small players or startups can do. Therefore, I think that things like what Ant Group has been doing before will likely involve collaboration with the CSRC, and it may be among the first in China to implement and launch applications in this direction. Therefore, I believe that either a large enough internet company with fintech attributes can do this, or some listed companies in China might allocate some projects for pilot projects. The initial compliance process and costs are actually very high. So I think if you're concerned about RWA, you can hold off for another six months or a year and see how things develop. Finally, regarding legal liability, this also addresses a concern many have raised since this news came out. One point is, what are the legal risks for individual investors investing in crypto assets or related financial products? This point isn't new; it remains the same: civil acts are invalid, and the risk is borne by the individual. Another point is that those suspected of disrupting financial order or endangering financial security will be investigated and dealt with by relevant departments according to law. There's a slightly subtle point here: "financial order" actually includes systems like foreign exchange management. This means that if you buy cryptocurrency purely for personal cross-border use, there will still be significant legal risks if discovered. Everyone should understand this, so we'll leave it at that. Besides individual players, we also know that a large number of KOLs and Chinese-speaking operational partners are actually active in mainland China. A friend told me that 40% of global cryptocurrency trading volume is currently conducted in mainland China. The exchanges that constantly talk about compliance, apart from the two in Hong Kong, mostly still conduct business face-to-face, a practice known as "selective compliance": applying for licenses overseas while simultaneously conducting similar activities in China. We won't judge this now, but what I really want to emphasize is that KOLs or content creators providing traffic and promotion services for these institutions should pay attention. I mentioned at an event recently that many partners feel it's not a big problem to post rebate links on foreign media like Twitter (formerly Twitter) to get users to register and receive rebates. However, in reality, the content and information we publish in foreign media will be noticed, and everyone should understand what that means. Therefore, for those of us in mainland China, whether through activities or links, helping some non-compliant overseas exchanges (here, "compliant" is a gray area term) to attract customers is a matter of probability and luck if it's a large, core platform; but if it's a smaller platform, people are attracted by its high commissions and promote it extensively. If, during subsequent law enforcement, the platform is found to be operating illegally, or even involved in gambling-related content, the criminal risk for the person attracting the traffic is very high. Therefore, regarding legal liability, in addition to focusing on individual holdings and investments, we also need to pay close attention to the partners who attract and promote these trading institutions or OTC merchants. This is something we particularly need to focus on. That concludes my nearly half-hour discussion of this regulatory document, along with the CSRC's Document No. 1, and some of my personal views. Of course, I didn't prepare a written transcript, so the expression may not be entirely precise, but I believe the core points and meanings have been conveyed. I hope this will be of some positive help to everyone's future business choices, overseas expansion strategies, or personal investments and transactions. For more detailed questions, please add me on WeChat for further discussion.

data.title: Binance Controls 87% of Trump-Backed USD1 as Stablecoin Surges to $5.4B
data.description:
Binance Controls 87% of Trump-Backed USD1 Stablecoin as Political and Market Stakes Rise

Binance now custodies a staggering 87% of the total supply of USD1, the flagship stablecoin issued by Trump family-backed World Liberty Financial (WLF), according to blockchain intelligence firm Arkham.

Out of USD1’s $5.4 billion circulating supply, approximately $4.7 billion is held in Binance-linked wallets — an unprecedented level of concentration among major global stablecoins and one that is rapidly drawing scrutiny across both financial and political circles.

Binance Controls 87% of Trump-Linked Stablecoin #USD1 🚨

Forbes reveals Binance is holding ~$4.7B of USD1, accounting for nearly 87% of the $5.4B circulating supply

its one of the highest single-exchange concentrations ever seen in a major stablecoin

$USD1 is issued by World… pic.twitter.com/RCK0az6lvU

— 🦧Mr. APE aka GEM Hunter💎 (@deg_ape) February 11, 2026

The custody dominance comes amid explosive growth, with USD1’s market capitalization surging 50% since January, transforming the token into one of the fastest-scaling stablecoins in the market.

USD1’s rapid expansion has been fueled by aggressive strategic moves. A $40 million WLFI token airdrop incentivized Binance users to hold the stablecoin on the exchange, while a landmark $2 billion investment by Abu Dhabi-backed MGX in May 2025 — executed in USD1 — cemented Binance as the ecosystem’s primary liquidity hub.

If you still think crypto is a fad, explain this:

MGX just put $2 billion into Binance—the biggest investment in crypto EVER.

Why? Because:

- AI + Blockchain = The financial revolution
- Binance dominates the industry
- Institutional money isn’t here to gamble—it’s here to… pic.twitter.com/pGoRvGA6cb

— Ak47♛ (@HolaItsAk47) March 13, 2025

The financial architecture behind the project adds another layer of intrigue. A Trump-affiliated entity reportedly owns 38% of World Liberty Financial and receives 75% of the venture’s net profits, generated by investing USD1 reserves into high-yield U.S. Treasuries currently returning around 3.6%.

Analysts estimate that USD1’s rapid scaling may have added nearly $1 billion to the Trump family’s net worth over the past year, underscoring how stablecoin infrastructure can translate directly into political-adjacent wealth generation.

🚨President Trump’s Net Worth Hits $6.5 Billion, Making Him One of World’s 500 Richest People

Thanks for the "Special Treatment"🤣🤣🤣

Trump's POLLS go up, net worth goes up, and Truth Social goes up 39%.

They will stop at nothing to GET Trump.

Vote for Trump! Prevent WWIII pic.twitter.com/csdPWigWKq

— AJ Huber (@Huberton) March 25, 2024

Binance, for its part, characterizes its role as “standard infrastructure support.” Yet critics warn that such extreme concentration creates systemic tail risk: any regulatory action, technical disruption, or legal challenge facing the exchange could reverberate across nearly the entire USD1 supply.

Ethics Questions Shadow White House and Exchange Ties

The concentration has intensified conflict-of-interest concerns, particularly following reports of a $500 million Emirati investment into WLF just days before the 2025 inauguration, granting a member of Abu Dhabi’s royal family a 49% stake in the venture.

Some lawmakers and watchdog groups have drawn connections between that investment and the subsequent presidential pardon of Binance founder Changpeng Zhao, allegations the White House has repeatedly dismissed. Officials maintain that the President’s business interests are held in a blind trust managed by his children, rejecting claims of “pay-to-play” politics.

Donald Trump doesn't even know who CZ is 👇
So why was CZ pardoned? pic.twitter.com/1vLeabNC72

— Tokio (@Tokio_nb) February 10, 2026

Still, the optics of an exchange holding 87% of a politically linked stablecoin’s supply remain a flashpoint as congressional oversight intensifies.

As World Liberty Financial prepares for its upcoming global forum at Mar-a-Lago on February 18, industry observers are watching closely for signs that the project may seek a U.S. national bank charter — a move that could diversify custody, formalize regulatory standing, and distance the venture from its reliance on the world’s largest offshore exchange.

For now, USD1 stands as a powerful — and controversial — symbol of crypto’s expanding intersection with capital markets, geopolitical capital flows, and executive power.

data.title: The Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) has approved margin financing for virtual assets to enhance market liquidity.
data.description: The Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) issued new guidelines on November 11, allowing licensed brokers (virtual asset brokers) providing virtual asset trading services to expand their related services to include margin financing. A high-level framework has also been established to provide guidance to virtual asset trading platforms, assisting them in developing recommendations for leveraged virtual asset-related products intended for professional investors. The SFC is expanding its product and service diversity in accordance with its ASPIRe roadmap. As one of the latest measures, the SFC now allows virtual asset brokers to provide virtual asset financing services to their securities margin clients, provided they have sufficient collateral and robust investor protection. This will encourage margin clients with sound credit conditions and collateral to participate more actively in virtual asset trading, thereby improving liquidity in the Hong Kong market within a controllable risk framework. For licensed virtual asset trading platforms, the SFC has for the first time developed a high-level framework to guide them in developing perpetual contracts for leveraged products available only to professional investors. This move aims to assist investors in implementing risk management strategies and improve the spot market liquidity of their underlying assets. To ensure investor protection, the framework outlines the high transparency, clear disclosure, and robust operational monitoring measures required for these leveraged products. To further promote virtual asset trading activities in Hong Kong, the Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) permits affiliates of licensed virtual asset trading platforms to act as market makers on their platforms, provided that strong safeguards are in place to reduce conflicts of interest. The participation of these affiliates should provide licensed virtual asset trading platforms with new liquidity channels. Dr. Yip Chi-hang, Executive Director of the Intermediaries Division of the Securities and Futures Commission (SFC), said: “The SFC’s step-by-step development approach, based on the ASPIRe roadmap, is crucial for the large-scale development of Hong Kong’s digital asset market. These targeted measures aim to enhance market liquidity and demonstrate the SFC’s strong commitment to developing Hong Kong’s digital asset market in a sustainable and collaborative manner.” The SFC will continue to closely monitor the implementation of these measures and maintain communication with stakeholders to ensure that they contribute to a safe and competitive market environment in Hong Kong.

data.title: RWA's new regulations open the floodgates—who will seize the new opportunity in cross-border financing?
data.description:
The "Notice" defines RWA tokenization as a new type of securities activity. Cross-border RWA tokenization is equivalent to cross-border securities activity, which means that securities intermediaries licensed in both mainland China and Hong Kong are expected to benefit. Domestic assets are expected to be able to conduct RWA tokenization issuance and trading in Hong Kong through compliant channels. Recently, the People's Bank of China (hereinafter referred to as the "PBOC") and eight other departments jointly issued the "Notice on Further Preventing and Handling Risks Related to Virtual Currencies" (hereinafter referred to as the "Notice"), which clarifies the regulations on virtual currencies and real-world assets (real-world assets). The characterization of RWA tokenization and related business activities, and related regulatory arrangements. The Notice explicitly prohibits the conduct of real-world asset tokenization activities within China, as well as the provision of related intermediary and information technology services; however, it also clarifies that, with the approval of the competent business authorities in accordance with laws and regulations, relevant business activities conducted based on specific financial infrastructure are exempt. Simultaneously, the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) released the "Regulatory Guidelines on the Issuance of Asset-Backed Securities Tokens Overseas by Domestic Assets" (hereinafter referred to as the "Guidelines"), further providing guidance for the issuance of asset-backed securities tokens overseas by domestic assets. The tokenization-related activities have been provided with relatively clear regulatory rules. The "Notice" and "Guidelines" are regarded by the industry as "new regulations" for WA. Several interviewees told Caixin that the new regulations actually leave a "very narrow loophole" for domestic assets to achieve cross-border financing through tokenization. The "Notice" defines RWA tokenization as a new type of securities activity, and cross-border RWA tokenization is equivalent to cross-border securities activity. Zou Chuanwei, Dean of the Jiangsu Provincial Institute of Financial Technology and Technology Finance, told Caixin that this means securities intermediaries licensed in both mainland China and Hong Kong are likely to benefit. The "Guidelines" are seen as a signal that regulators are clarifying gray areas. "Regulation will not be allowed to circumvent existing rules regarding securities, foreign exchange, data security, and censorship. Instead, it tokenizes cross-border RWA." "Bringing the issue back from the gray area to the rule of law emphasizes that it should be verifiable, accountable, and transparent," Zhao Binghao, Dean of the Institute of Financial Technology and Law at China University of Political Science and Law, told Caixin. "The timing of these two documents was unexpected, and it's speculated that it's related to the fact that Hong Kong officially issued stablecoin licenses in early March," an industry insider told Caixin. As the central bank and eight other departments clarified regulatory rules, the Hong Kong SAR government released a strong signal to further promote the development of digital assets. On February 2nd, Hong Kong Chief Executive John Lee announced at a public event that Hong Kong will issue its first batch of stablecoin issuer licenses next month. At the same event, Ip Chi-hang, Executive Director of the Intermediaries Division of the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission, stated that this year's focus is on improving market liquidity. To this end, the Hong Kong Securities and Futures Commission will allow affiliated market makers (referring to related companies of VATP) to operate on licensed virtual asset trading platforms (VATP), allowing the platform's affiliated companies to provide liquidity. "Previously, the market was worried that mainland assets couldn't be used for RWA (Research, Investment, and Development) overseas, limiting Hong Kong to a single city with little market space. Now, with the approval of the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC), related business activities can be carried out based on specific financial infrastructure. This is beneficial for assets with stable cash flow and long return cycles," said the aforementioned industry insider.

Three categories of cross-borderRWABusiness is feasible

Licensed securities firms in both Hong Kong and mainland China are expected to benefit

Hong Kong is a key location for mainland entities to conduct business overseasRWA，and Xunying Mobility's battery swapping assetsRWAetc。 This has led to the formation of a cross-border RWA tokenization model consisting of "Mainland China assets + consortium blockchain + Hong Kong regulatory sandbox". In response, Zhao Binghao cautioned that under the new regulations, the Hong Kong Sandbox remains a "controlled testing ground," but it cannot be used as an "exemption card." The industry needs to prioritize compliance design, such as targeting only qualified institutional investors, transparent disclosure and auditing, segregation of funds and assets, establishing auditable data boundaries and external service boundaries within China, and fulfilling filing procedures to ensure that "no issuance or trading occurs domestically, and no overseas promotion is conducted domestically." Against this backdrop, Zhao Binghao believes that the likely beneficiaries this time are not "the Web3 companies that are best at telling stories," but rather institutions that can build a solid compliance infrastructure, including licensed financial institutions and the custody of the securitization chain. The new regulations also emphasize strengthening the management of financial, intermediary, and information technology service institutions. This includes infrastructure service providers such as registration and clearing companies, as well as professional institutions capable of providing transparent disclosure, auditing, data security, and compliance technology capabilities. The "Notice" clarifies that overseas subsidiaries and branches of domestic financial institutions providing services related to the tokenization of real-world assets overseas should be included in the compliance and risk control management system of the domestic financial institutions, and requirements regarding customer access, suitability management, and anti-money laundering should be strictly implemented. Furthermore, intermediary institutions and information technology service institutions that provide services for domestic entities to directly or indirectly conduct real-world asset tokenization business in the form of foreign debt overseas, or to conduct real-world asset tokenization business overseas based on domestic equity, must establish and improve relevant compliance and internal control systems, strengthen business and risk management, and report or file their business operations in accordance with relevant requirements. The aforementioned industry insider believes that this will benefit intermediary agencies that can build bridges between Hong Kong and the mainland. In 2025, overseas subsidiaries of Chinese securities firms such as Guotai Junan International (1788.HK) and Tianfeng International announced upgrades to their Hong Kong securities licenses, gaining permission to provide services such as virtual asset trading and consulting. According to Wind data, following the release of new regulatory rules on February 6th, the Hong Kong stock index for Chinese securities firms rose for two consecutive days, with Guotai Junan International rising over 9% in three trading days. It is worth noting that several interviewees stated that the "Notice" and "Guidelines" provide preliminary regulatory rules, but more details need to be further clarified by relevant regulatory departments. "Currently, the China Securities Regulatory Commission has issued the 'Guidelines,' but cross-border RWA tokenization business still involves cross-border funds and data, and further clear guidance from relevant regulatory authorities is needed," said Zou Chuanwei. The red lines in the "Notice" are drawn quite rigidly. If the definition of "specific financial infrastructure" and the approval process are not transparent, [failure to comply with regulations will result in consequences]. "It's possible that compliant innovation will be hampered by uncertainty," Zhao Binghao told Caixin. "I suggest that the China Securities Regulatory Commission and other regulatory departments should provide an operational whitelist, process, and boundary explanation as soon as possible to avoid..." The recurring cycle of 'banning it all/letting it go leads to chaos'

data.title: China's "crypto winter" is getting colder.
data.description:
Author: Suvashree Ghosh, Bloomberg; Translation: Saoirse, Foresight News

The global crypto market has been sluggish recently, and China has tightened its regulations on cryptocurrencies and stablecoins again, explicitly prohibiting the issuance of RMB-pegged stablecoins overseas without approval, directly impacting Hong Kong's development as a digital asset center. This article focuses on the market reaction and industry impact after the policy's implementation, revealing the core contradiction between capital controls and crypto innovation. Against the backdrop of intensified industry reshuffling and accelerated capital withdrawal, the crypto sector is returning to pragmatic development, and the relevant regulatory boundaries and future trends deserve continued attention.

November 26, 2025, a cryptocurrency exchange store in Hong Kong.

Photography: Lam Yik/Bloomberg

Digital Sector Setback

Last year, a growing number of crypto industry commentators believed that China's attitude towards digital assets might be turning around.

After Pan Gongsheng, governor of the People's Bank of China, expressed his vision that the renminbi could challenge the dominance of the US dollar, there were constant rumors of a "policy easing" in the market.

But on February 7, all these expectations came to an abrupt end.

During the latest round of cryptocurrency crashes, China tightened its regulations on cryptocurrencies and the tokenization of physical assets, prohibiting domestic institutions from issuing digital tokens overseas and banning the issuance of renminbi-pegged stablecoins overseas without approval. Officials stated that this move was to prevent currency sovereignty risks.

"China's attitude toward stablecoins has been at best tentative, and has become increasingly lukewarm in recent months," said Angela Ang, Head of Policy and Strategic Partnerships for Asia Pacific at blockchain intelligence firm TRM Labs. She added that the central bank's announcement "completely dashes any hope of launching an offshore RMB stablecoin in the short term—definitely not for Hong Kong, and likely for other regions as well." This is a major setback for Hong Kong and its long-standing goal of becoming a digital asset center. Last June, Hong Kong's Secretary for Financial Services and the Treasury, Christopher Hui, stated that he did not rule out the possibility of pegging a Hong Kong stablecoin to the RMB based on regulatory requirements. Now, it is widely believed that he has completely closed that door. During Trump's presidency, the supply of dollar-denominated stablecoins surged. Source: Artemis Analytics. As Angela Ang stated, this tightening regulatory signal was already evident. As early as August of last year, China had already required local securities firms and related institutions to stop publishing research reports on stablecoins and holding related promotional seminars to curb overheated market sentiment. Patrick Tan, general counsel for blockchain intelligence firm ChainArgos, stated that last week's policy announcement "removed the uncertainty hanging over the market regarding the private issuance of RMB stablecoins. Issuers now know exactly where the red lines are." Institutions applying for licenses will now have to focus on issuing stablecoins pegged to the Hong Kong dollar. Bloomberg News previously reported that as many as 50 companies in Hong Kong planned to apply for stablecoin licenses last year, including tech giants Ant Group and JD.com. However, according to a Financial Times report in October, these companies were forced to suspend their stablecoin plans after Beijing intervened. Neither Ant Group nor JD.com responded to requests for comment. As of Tuesday, Hong Kong had issued licenses to 11 cryptocurrency exchanges and approved 62 companies to conduct digital asset trading for clients. The list includes Chinese-backed institutions such as CMB International Securities Limited, Guotai Junan Securities (Hong Kong) Limited, and Tianfu Futures Limited. However, industry insiders worry that without access to the RMB, the entire plan may be in vain. "The problem has never been Hong Kong's regulatory framework, but rather whether China will tolerate the circulation of RMB-denominated instruments outside its control," said Patrick Tan. "Capital controls and stablecoin liberalization are fundamentally mutually exclusive." Market data continues to weaken. Bitcoin perpetual futures open interest continues to decline. Since its decline began last October, Bitcoin perpetual futures open interest has failed to rebound, highlighting the lack of confidence supporting this current rebound. Coinglass data shows that it has fallen by about 50% from its October high.

Fund outflows: $3.3 billion

Bloomberg Intelligence compiled data showing that since the plunge in early October, investors have withdrawn approximately $3.3 billion from the US Ethereum ETF, with year-to-date withdrawals exceeding $500 million. Data shows that the Ethereum ETF's assets have fallen below $13 billion, the lowest level since July of last year.

Industry Perspective: "The market is consolidating around truly effective areas. Even well-funded crypto-native VCs are heavily shifting towards fintech, stablecoin businesses, and prediction markets, leaving other areas struggling to gain attention." — Santiago Roel Santos, Founder and CEO of crypto private equity firm Inversion. Crypto venture capital funds are shifting their focus to higher-performing areas such as stablecoin infrastructure and on-chain prediction markets, and expanding into related adjacent sectors.

data.title: US Mobile Giant T-Mobile Turns Any Phone Call Into a Live AI Translator Supporting 50+ Languages Without Apps
data.description:
Imagine a world where your phone does not just carry your voice but also understands the meaning behind it.

T-Mobile, the American telecommunications giant known for its "Un-carrier" branding and extensive 5G footprint, is attempting to dismantle language barriers by embedding artificial intelligence directly into its infrastructure.

By moving the "brain" of the operation from the device to the network, the company aims to turn every call into a potential bridge between cultures.

.@TMobile launches live translation for 50+ languages powered by AI integrated into its core network infrastructure - no app needed 🤯

The innovation engine continues to hum and I expect this is just the start with other AI enabled services to follow $TMUSpic.twitter.com/CkhsWg2thI

— Will Townsend (@WillTownTech) February 11, 2026

How Does AI Living In The Network Change The Game?

Most translation tools today feel like an awkward middleman – you need a specific app, a subscription, or a high-end handset to make them work.

T-Mobile is flipping this script by integrating an "agentic" AI platform into its 5G Advanced network.

This means the intelligence is native to the signal itself.

Inspiring update from @SriniGopalan at @TMobile’s Capital Markets Day.

Our network investments are delivering real customer impact, including breakthrough 5G innovations, AI-powered Live Translation and emerging possibilities as we look ahead to 6G. This is network leadership… pic.twitter.com/58XluVVpcu

— John Saw (@JohnSaw) February 11, 2026

John Saw, T-Mobile’s President of Technology and CTO explained,

“The intelligence is built into our network, not in the phones.”

Because it lives in the IMS (IP Multimedia Subsystem) network, it works on everything from the latest flagship smartphones to basic flip phones.

The service is designed to be instantaneous, using the low latency of 5G Standalone technology to ensure conversations feel natural rather than robotic.

Can Dialling A Simple Code Bridge 50 Languages?

Starting today, eligible customers can register for a free beta trial set to begin this spring.

Never get lost in translation again. With Live Translation built into the network, you can connect with anyone in 50+ languages. Register for the beta today at https://t.co/y5HeUjymn3pic.twitter.com/gpE5gp5pCu

— T-Mobile (@TMobile) February 11, 2026

The process is remarkably low-tech for such high-tech software: users simply dial *87* during an active call to trigger the AI.

Once active, the system can automatically detect and translate over 50 different languages in real-time.

Srini Gopalan, CEO of T-Mobile, noted that this is about more than just tech; it is about the 60 million Americans in multilingual households.

“By bringing real-time AI directly into our network, we’re delivering more than connectivity — turning conversations into community.”

A language barrier shouldn’t be the reason a call doesn’t happen. That’s what led us to build Live Translation, supporting 50+ languages, directly into @TMobile's network.

Using network-native AI, calls are translated in real time, and it all happens live within our network – no… pic.twitter.com/4UH0MtRmRP

— Srini Gopalan (@SriniGopalan) February 11, 2026

Whether it is a grandmother speaking to a grandson in a different tongue or a traveller making a dinner reservation in a foreign city, the goal is to prevent missed opportunities caused by language gaps.

Does This Move Affect Your Privacy And Data?

One of the most pressing questions with any AI listening to a live call is: who else is hearing this?

T-Mobile has been firm on its stance regarding security.

The service is designed to translate and then "move on."

According to Saw,

“The service is designed to translate conversations in real time and then move on, without storing the content of those calls.”

No recordings or transcripts are saved, and the AI models are reportedly not trained on customer data, but rather on millions of internal test calls.

Is T-Mobile Shifting The Industry’s Financial Future?

This innovation comes as T-Mobile projects a massive growth trajectory for 2026.

The company expects service revenues to hit $77 billion this year, climbing to over $80 billion by 2027.

With a target of adding 900,000 to 1 million postpaid net accounts in 2026, the "Un-carrier" is betting that unique features like Live Translation will widen its lead over competitors.

The company is also prioritising shareholders, with plans to double its share repurchases to $5 billion in the first quarter of 2026.

By turning the network into a flexible platform, T-Mobile can "plug in" different AI models as they improve, ensuring they aren't locked into a single vendor's limitations.

The Death Of The Language Barrier Or The Rise Of The Global Listener?

Coinlive views this as a definitive shift from the "dumb pipe" era of telecommunications to a "cognitive network" reality.

If a wireless provider can effectively commoditise fluency, they stop selling mere minutes and start selling human understanding.

T-Mobile just announced network-level live call translation!

It works on any phone on the T-Mobile network. We'll have to see how it compares to live translation features on some phones and how accurate it is.

Register here: https://t.co/sGj5KrzCUnpic.twitter.com/LNbNaEDYyE

— Max (@maxhietpas_) February 11, 2026

While this move empowers millions of multilingual families and global travellers, it raises a provocative question: as we outsource our comprehension to a network-level AI, do we risk losing the human drive to actually learn and connect through effort?

If T-Mobile succeeds, expect a domino effect where global telcos are forced to move beyond data speeds and into the business of digital cognition.

This is no longer about how fast you can download; it is about how well you can be understood.
